pattern: Any row only ever needs its own top-3 cells as rook candidates, because at most two other rooks can block two of those three columns.
hints: For a single row, if two other rooks already occupy two columns, the best available cell in that row is always among that row's three highest-valued cells - a lower-ranked cell can never beat all three of them being blocked at once.
So reduce every row to its top-3 (value, column) candidates, then the problem becomes choosing one candidate from each of three distinct rows with three distinct columns.

Precompute each row's top-3 cells, enumerate every triple of distinct rows, and for each triple try the (at most 27) combinations of one candidate per row, keeping the max sum where the three columns are all different.

edge_inputs.description: 3x3 all-negative board still forces three distinct rows/cols
rectangular board, more columns than rows
rectangular board, more rows than columns
one column holds every row's best value, forcing two rooks off their row maximum
two columns tie for best across all rows
minimum board size, all identical values
moderately large square board with varied values

stress.description: random board, side capped for tractable verification-time execution
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = max(3, size)
    n = max(3, size)
    board = [[rng.randint(-1000000000, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]
    return [board]

complexity.time: O(m^3) with a constant-27 inner loop over top-3 candidates
